Julian de Angelis at MercadoLibre has been deploying AI coding agents across nearly 20,000 developers and thousands of repositories. His conclusion after operating at that scale:

Your AI coding agent is a brilliant new hire on day one. Fast, eager, and with zero context about your codebase, your conventions, or your architecture.

You wouldn’t hand a new engineer a Jira ticket that says “fix this bug” and walk away. You’d onboard them: docs, tech stack overview, style guides, release workflows, access to internal tools, a mentor to review their work.

That onboarding system is what de Angelis calls the agent harness - the structured set of controls that shape what the agent knows, what it can do, and how its work gets validated. The underlying discipline is context engineering: designing and controlling everything an LLM sees before it generates a single token.

The Constraint Nobody Talks About

Every popular AI coding agent - Claude Code, Cursor, Codex - runs the same fundamental loop:

Read -> Plan -> Code -> Validate -> Iterate

The agent reads your files, plans an approach, writes code, runs tests, and loops back if something fails. At every iteration, the context window grows. System prompts, tool definitions, conversation history, file contents, terminal output, the agent’s own generated code - all of it accumulates in a finite workspace.

Here’s the part that surprises people: past roughly 60% utilization of the context window, more context makes the agent actively worse. This phenomenon is called context rot. Even with windows reaching 1M tokens, the fundamental challenge remains. Every token spent on a verbose tool result or an overly long rules file is a token not available for the actual task.

The best agent harnesses are designed with this reality at the center: inject the right context at the right moment, and avoid polluting the window with anything else.

Four Levers That Actually Matter

1. Custom Rules

Files like CLAUDE.md, cursor/rules, or AGENTS.md get injected into the agent’s context at the start of every interaction. This is the most accessible lever.

What goes in:

  • Tech stack and architecture patterns
  • Naming conventions and code style
  • Testing philosophy (“always write unit tests with table-driven inputs”)
  • Common pitfalls specific to your codebase
  • Anti-patterns you’ve seen the agent produce repeatedly

What doesn’t belong:

  • Entire API documentation (too long, wastes context)
  • Obvious instructions (“write clean code”)
  • Contradictory rules

Practical tips:

  • Keep them under 500 lines. Precision matters.
  • Split rules into focused files by concern: architecture, testing, code style, security. The agent loads only what’s relevant.
  • Include few-shot examples. Models learn from examples better than abstract instructions.
  • Make them living documents. Every time the agent makes a mistake, update the rules. Over weeks, this file becomes remarkably effective.

2. MCP Servers

MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers are plugins that give the agent tools beyond reading and writing files. Without them, the agent is limited to what’s in the repo. With them, it can:

  • Query your database to understand schema and data
  • Search internal documentation or wikis
  • Look up API contracts
  • Interact with CI/CD pipelines
  • Access design specs from Figma
  • Validate its own output against real systems

MCP servers bridge the gap between the agent and your organization’s knowledge. They give the agent access to the same resources a human developer would use.

3. Skills

Skills give the agent new capabilities and domain expertise. A skill is a directory with an entrypoint file that can bundle templates, example outputs, reference docs, and executable scripts.

The design is clever: only a short description stays in context permanently, so the agent knows what’s available. The full content loads only when the skill is invoked - by the user or automatically when the agent detects relevance. This on-demand loading means skills can be as detailed as needed without permanently consuming context budget.

They come in two flavors:

  • Reference skills inject knowledge: conventions, patterns, domain context
  • Task skills provide step-by-step instructions for specific actions

The real power is that skills can bundle and execute scripts, run in isolated subagents with their own clean context window, and compose together. Programmable, context-aware, composable units of agent behavior.

4. Spec-Driven Development

This one reframes how you interact with agents entirely.

Consider this prompt: “Make a new feature to add new items from the backoffice.”

Seems simple. But it says nothing about: which tech stack, where the backoffice is, API contracts, storage layer, idempotency handling, authorization rules.

The agent will read the codebase, plan something reasonable, and produce a working MVP. You add an item and it works. You click “add” again and it inserts the item twice. It was “obvious” the agent should have managed idempotency by ID. In your business, it’s “obvious” that only one role can insert items.

The problem isn’t the agent. It’s the bare initial input. The bottleneck sits between the chair and the screen.

Spec-Driven Development (SDD) solves this by writing detailed specifications before the agent writes code. What the feature does, how it integrates, edge cases, acceptance criteria, test plans. The spec becomes the harness - it consolidates architecture decisions, guidance, and review criteria into a single artifact that engineers the agent’s entire context window in one shot.

And you can use the agent to write the specs too.

The Feedback Loop

The four levers define what the agent knows and can do. The piece that makes the whole system converge is the feedback loop: tests, linters, type checkers, build scripts. Every tool that produces a pass/fail signal becomes a self-correction mechanism.

Agent hooks make this enforceable. They are user-defined commands that execute automatically at specific points in the agent’s lifecycle. Wire your validation into a Stop hook, and the agent literally cannot finish until the checks pass. Not a suggestion it might ignore. An enforced gate.

The more structured feedback available, the less human intervention is needed.

MercadoLibre’s Playbook at 20,000 Developers

Theory is fine. Here’s what works at scale with 9+ technology stacks, thousands of legacy repositories, and multiple knowledge domains:

Standardized rules per technology. A curated set of custom rules at the org level, tuned to each tech stack, internal libraries, and code review standards. Teams inherit the base and layer on repo-specific rules. Getting the specificity right is critical - too specific breaks legacy projects, too loose lets unapproved libraries through (security risk).

Internal MCP servers. Built-in access to internal cloud platform capabilities, internal SDKs, business context via RAG, and updated application documentation.

Custom code review agents. Standalone review agents running in CI that analyze every PR - human-written or AI-generated - before a human reviewer sees it. They return prioritized findings so engineers see critical issues first. Human reviewers focus on design decisions instead of catching style violations. Faster review cycles, more consistent quality, and a safety net that scales with PR volume, not reviewer headcount.

Start Here

If you haven’t built an agent harness yet, start with the simplest lever: custom rules. Create a CLAUDE.md or equivalent file with your coding conventions, common pitfalls, and a couple of concrete examples of good and bad patterns in your codebase.

Run it for a week. Every time the agent produces something wrong, add a rule. By the end of the month, that file will have quietly transformed your results more than any model upgrade could.

The teams that win with AI coding agents won’t be the ones using the fanciest model. They’ll be the ones with the best-engineered harness.

Based on Julian de Angelis’s analysis of AI coding agent deployment at MercadoLibre. Original thread.

item.title: WebReel: Stop Re-Recording Your Demo Videos
item.content:encoded:
WebReel: Stop Re-Recording Your Demo Videos

You’ve spent 45 minutes recording a product demo. The cursor wobbled on the third click. A Slack notification popped up mid-take. Now you’re on attempt number six, and the “Get Started” button has moved two pixels since the last deploy.

If you’ve ever needed to create browser demos — for product pages, changelogs, tutorials, or investor decks — you know this pain. Screen recording sounds simple and turns out to be maddening. The mouse never moves smoothly enough. The timing is always off. And the moment your UI ships a change, every video goes stale.

Vercel Labs released WebReel to solve exactly this.

What is WebReel?

WebReel is an open-source CLI tool that records browser demos from JSON configuration files. You define what should happen — clicks, keystrokes, scrolling, typing, dragging — and WebReel handles the rest.

Under the hood: WebReel launches a headless Chrome instance, navigates to your URL, executes each step in sequence, captures screenshots at roughly 60 frames per second, composites cursor animations and keystroke overlays on each frame, and encodes the final output with ffmpeg. Both Chrome and ffmpeg are downloaded automatically on first use.

GitHub: vercel-labs/webreel Documentation: webreel.dev License: Apache 2.0 Version: 0.1.4 Author: Chris Tate (Vercel Labs)

Quick Start

Three commands to go from zero to a professional demo video:

The init command scaffolds a webreel.config.json with a JSON schema for IDE autocompletion:

Key Features

12 action types cover most demo scenarios:

ActionWhat it does
pauseWait for a duration
clickMove cursor to an element and click
keyPress a key or key combo (e.g. cmd+z)
typeType text character by character
scrollScroll the page or a specific element
waitWait for an element to appear
screenshotCapture a PNG screenshot mid-flow
dragDrag from one element to another
moveToMove cursor to an element without clicking
navigateNavigate to a new URL mid-video
hoverTrigger CSS hover states
selectSelect a value in a dropdown

Multiple output formats: MP4 (default), GIF, and WebM (VP9).

Customizable overlays: Cursor animations and keystroke HUD can be themed via the theme config option.

Multi-video configs: Define multiple videos in a single config file, each producing its own output.

Shared steps: Use include to share common setup steps (like dismissing a cookie banner) across videos.

Scenarios and Best Practices

Product demos that stay current

The biggest win: your demo video is now code. Commit webreel.config.json to your repo. When the UI changes, update the config and re-record. Review demo changes in pull requests just like you review code.

CI/CD integration

Add webreel record to your build pipeline. Every deploy generates fresh demo videos that match the current state of your product. No more “the button moved but the demo still shows the old layout.”

Changelog videos

Ship a feature? Write a quick WebReel config showing it in action. The config is small, reviewable, and produces consistent output every time.

Tutorial and onboarding flows

Script multi-step onboarding flows with visible cursor movement and keystroke labels. Users see exactly where to click and what to type.

Screenshot generation

Use the screenshot action to capture PNGs at specific points during a flow. Useful for generating documentation images alongside videos.

Mobile viewport recording

Set viewport to mobile dimensions (e.g., 375x812) to record demos at phone screen sizes.

CLI Commands

CommandPurpose
webreel initScaffold a new config file
webreel recordRecord videos from config
webreel previewRun steps in a visible browser (no recording)
webreel compositeRe-render from stored raw recordings
webreel validateCheck config for errors
webreel installDownload Chrome and ffmpeg

FAQ

Do I need to install Chrome or ffmpeg separately? No. WebReel downloads both automatically to ~/.webreel on first use. Use webreel install --force if binaries get corrupted.

Can I target elements by CSS selector? Yes. Actions accept either text (matches visible text) or selector (CSS selector). You can also scope with within to target elements inside a specific container.

What about pages that require authentication? You can use shared steps (include) to script a login flow, or navigate to a page that’s already authenticated via cookies.

Can I record multiple videos from one config? Yes. The videos object maps video names to their configs. Each produces its own output file.

What Node.js version is required? Node.js v18 or later.

Comparison with Alternatives

ToolApproachReproducibleCI/CD ReadyCursor/Keystroke Overlays
WebReelJSON-scripted headless browserYesYesBuilt-in
OBS / ScreenFlowManual screen recordingNoNoManual post-production
Playwright + ffmpegCustom scriptingPartiallyYesDIY
Loom / Screen StudioCloud recordingNoNoLimited
AsciinemaTerminal recording onlyYesYesN/A (terminal only)

WebReel occupies a specific niche: it’s for browser-based demos that need to be reproducible, automated, and polished without manual editing.

References

  • WebReel GitHub Repository
  • WebReel Documentation
  • WebReel Examples
  • npm: webreel

item.title: NVIDIA: Agentic AI Inflection Point — Inference Demand Explosion Is the Real Super Cycle, But ASIC Substitution Risk Is Being Underestimated
item.content:encoded:
$NVDA: Agentic AI Inflection Point — Inference Demand Explosion Is the Real Super Cycle, But ASIC Substitution Risk Is Being Underestimated

Executive Summary

NVIDIA delivered another blowout quarter in Q4 FY2026: revenue of $68.1B (+73% YoY), data center revenue of $62.3B (+75% YoY), and net income of $43.0B (+94% YoY). Full-year FY2026 revenue reached $215.9B with free cash flow of $96.6B — one of the most stunning growth curves in business history. Q1 FY2027 guidance of $78.0B significantly beat Wall Street’s $72.8B expectation.

Yet behind this seemingly perfect report, the stock dropped ~5.5% after-hours, erasing ~$260B in market cap. This tells us: the current price already prices in “perfect execution” with minimal margin for error.

TL;DR:

  • Recommended Action: HOLD / Buy on pullbacks — Current price (~$177) implies Forward PE of ~22-30x, not unreasonable but not cheap either. Confidence level: Medium-High
  • Core Key Force: AI Inference demand is growing exponentially, taking over from Training as NVIDIA’s primary revenue engine (>70% of data center revenue from inference)
  • Biggest Risk / Kill Condition: Custom ASIC chips (Google TPU, Amazon Trainium, Broadcom custom chips) accelerating share gains in the inference market. If ASIC inference share exceeds 30% in 2026, NVIDIA’s pricing power faces material pressure
  • Valuation vs Current Price: Base case fair value $190-$220, implied IRR 15-25%; Bear case $120-$140

Key Forces

Key Force #1: Exponential Inference Demand Growth — From “Training Arms Race” to “Inference = Revenue”

Jensen Huang’s core thesis from the earnings call: “In the new world of AI, compute equals revenues. Without compute, there’s no token generation; without tokens, there’s no revenue growth.”

This isn’t empty rhetoric. Over 70% of NVIDIA’s data center revenue comes from inference workloads, not training. The rise of Agentic AI means every user interaction requires massive inference compute — fundamentally different from the traditional SaaS model of “train once, use repeatedly.”

Grace Blackwell + NVLink architecture achieves “an order of magnitude reduction in cost per token” on the inference side — a genuine product moat. The Vera Rubin platform (expected H2 2026 production) will further train MoE models with one-quarter the GPUs, reducing inference token costs by another 10x.

Quantitative Evidence: CFO Colette Kress revealed $500B in Blackwell and Rubin order visibility from early 2025 through end of 2026.

Key Force #2: ASIC Substitution — The “Trojan Horse” of the Inference Market

NVIDIA’s dominance in training (>95% share) is hard to challenge near-term. But inference is a different battlefield:

  • Google TPU 7th-gen Ironwood approaches or partially surpasses Blackwell on technical specs
  • Broadcom signed a $21B TPU chip supply agreement with Anthropic
  • Custom ASIC shipments projected to grow 44.6% in 2026, far exceeding GPU’s 16.1% growth
  • Inference is more cost-sensitive; custom ASICs can deeply optimize for specific model architectures

Anthropic’s announcement of training Claude on 1 million TPUs is a landmark event, signaling top AI labs are seriously evaluating non-NVIDIA alternatives.

Key Force #3: China Export Controls — The “Half-Open, Half-Closed” $200B TAM

FY2025 China revenue was ~13% of total. Q1 FY2027 guidance completely excludes China data center compute revenue.

But the situation is evolving: the Trump administration allowed H20 chips with 15% tariff export to China in August 2025, expanded to H200 with 25% tariff in December. Blackwell remains banned. This creates new revenue opportunities but with extreme policy uncertainty.

A. Revenue Scale & Quality

A1. Revenue Breakdown

SegmentQ4 FY2026 RevenueYoYQoQShare
Data Center$62.3B+75%+22%91.5%
Gaming$3.7B-11%-22%5.4%
Professional Visualization$1.3B+159%+74%1.9%
Automotive$604M+6%—0.9%

A2. Growth Trajectory

QuarterTotal RevenueYoY
Q1 FY2026$44.0B+69%
Q2 FY2026$50.0B+122%
Q3 FY2026$56.7B+94%
Q4 FY2026$68.1B+73%
FY2026$215.9B+65%
Q1 FY2027 Guidance$78.0B~+77%

Growth remains extraordinary in absolute terms, but the second derivative is decelerating — from +114% YoY (FY2025) to +65% YoY (FY2026).

B. Profitability & Margins

B1. Margin Tracking

MetricQ3 FY2026Q4 FY2026QoQ ChangeFY2026
Non-GAAP Gross Margin73.6%75.2%+160bp~74.5%
GAAP Gross Margin73.4%75.0%+160bp—
Non-GAAP Operating Margin66.2%67.7%+150bp—
Non-GAAP Net Margin—~63%——

Q4 gross margin recovery (73.6% to 75.2%) addressed concerns about Blackwell early-stage yield issues.

B2. EPS

MetricQ4 FY2026EstimateBeat
Non-GAAP EPS$1.62$1.51+7.3%
GAAP EPS$1.76——

C. Cash Flow & Capital Allocation

C1. Cash Flow — “Money Printer” Level

MetricFY2026
Operating Cash Flow~$102.7B
Free Cash Flow~$96.6B
FCF Margin~44.7%

$96.6B in free cash flow means NVIDIA generates ~$265M in free cash every day.

C2. Shareholder Returns

  • FY2026 buybacks: $40.1B (41.5% of FCF)
  • Dividends: $974M
  • $60B buyback authorization remaining

D. Forward Guidance

DimensionQ4 ActualStreet Est.BeatQ1 FY2027 GuideStreet Est.Beat
Revenue$68.1B$66.2B+2.9%$78.0B +/-2%$72.8B+7.1%
Non-GAAP EPS$1.62$1.51+7.3%———
Gross Margin75.2%——75.0% +/-50bp——

E. Competitive Landscape

E1. Market Position

  • Training market: NVIDIA share >95%, near-monopoly
  • Inference market: NVIDIA share ~70%, facing rapid ASIC growth
  • ASIC growth 44.6% vs GPU growth 16.1%: If sustained, fundamentally changes competitive dynamics in 3-5 years

E2. Moat Assessment

Moat DimensionStatusTrend
CUDA Software EcosystemVery StrongSlow erosion (JAX/XLA/Triton)
Full-Stack IntegrationVery StrongWidening (DGX SuperPOD)
Scale Economies & TSMCStrongStable
Customer Switching CostsMedium-HighDeclining on inference side

K. Valuation Matrix

K1. Current Valuation

MetricValue
Price (~Feb 2026)~$177
Market Cap~$4.3T
Forward PE (FY2027E)~22-30x
EV/Revenue (TTM)~20x
FCF Yield (TTM)~2.2%

K2. Scenario Analysis

ScenarioProbTargetImplied ReturnKey Assumption
Bull30%$280+58%FY2027 rev >$350B, inference accelerates
Base50%$210+19%FY2027 rev ~$310B, growth slows to 40-50%
Bear20%$130-27%ASIC substitution accelerates, growth <30%

item.title: Palantir (PLTR): AI Operating System Thesis is Real — But the Market Already Knows It
item.content:encoded:
$PLTR: The “AI Operating System” Thesis is Real — But the Market Already Knows It

Executive Summary

Verdict: PASS at current levels. Action Price: $85-95 for initial position.

Palantir delivered what CEO Alex Karp called “the best results in tech in the last decade” — Q4 2025 revenue of $1.41B (+70% YoY), US commercial revenue exploding +137% YoY, and FY2026 guidance of $7.19B (+61%) that obliterated consensus by $970M. The business is genuinely exceptional: 85% gross margins, 57% adjusted operating margins, $7.2B net cash, zero debt, and a Rule of 40 score of 127%.

The problem is price, not business quality. At ~200x trailing P/E and 76x EV/Revenue, PLTR trades at 5-7x the multiple of its next most expensive comp (Snowflake at 13.5x). The stock is priced for perfection across a decade — any deceleration from 60%+ growth to even 40% would trigger severe multiple compression.

TL;DR

  • PASS at $137-146 — IRR insufficient to compensate for valuation risk.
  • Key Force: AIP/Ontology creating genuine “AI operating system” moat with 139% NDR.
  • Kill Condition: US Commercial revenue growth decelerating below 50% YoY for 2 consecutive quarters.
  • Action Price: $85-95 (implies ~55x 2026 EV/Revenue — still premium, but adequate IRR).

Key Forces (Decisive Factors)

Key Force #1: AIP + Ontology = The AI Operating System Moat

The Ontology is a semantic layer translating raw enterprise data into “Objects” and “Actions” for AI agents. Unlike competitors (Snowflake, Databricks) who store/query data, Palantir allows LLMs to take governed, auditable actions on real processes.

Evidence:

  • NDR: 139% in Q4 (+500bps QoQ).
  • US Commercial TCV: $1.34B in Q4 (+67% YoY).
  • AIP Bootcamps: 1,300+ completed; conversion rates of 5-10% drive pipeline velocity.

Key Force #2: US Government AI Spending Super-Cycle

Palantir is the primary beneficiary of increased government AI spending driven by China competition, DOGE-driven modernization, and active military conflicts.

Evidence:

  • US Army: $10B enterprise agreement (Summer 2025).
  • US Navy: $448M ShipOS deal (Dec 2025).
  • US Gov Revenue: Grew 66% YoY in Q4 to $570M.

Key Force #3: Extreme Valuation vs. Exceptional Execution

At ~$140/share, the market assumes $11-12B revenue by 2030 and sustained 40%+ margins. Any break in these assumptions risks a 40-60% downside via multiple compression.

A. Revenue Scale & Quality

item.title: VSDD: When Your AI Writes Code, Who Checks Its Homework?
item.content:encoded:
The Problem Nobody Talks About

Your AI coding assistant wrote a function. It generated tests. The tests pass. The code review looks clean.

Ship it?

Here’s what I keep seeing: six months later, a user hits an edge case that nobody — human or AI — anticipated. The function handled null inputs fine but choked on concurrent access. The tests covered the happy path and two sad paths but missed the degenerate case where the input was valid but pathologically large.

The AI didn’t write bad code. It wrote almost correct code. And “almost correct” is harder to catch than “obviously wrong” because it passes every check designed to catch obvious problems.

What Everyone Assumed

The industry settled into a comfortable pattern with AI coding tools:

  1. Describe what you want
  2. AI writes code (and maybe tests)
  3. You review it, tweak it, ship it

Some teams added TDD discipline. Some added code review by a second AI or a human. But the fundamental assumption remained: if the code passes tests and looks reasonable to a reviewer, it’s good enough.

That assumption has a blind spot.

The Blind Spot: AI Politeness

Standard LLM interactions are cooperative by design. The AI wants to help you. It wants to agree with you. When it reviews code — even its own code — it starts from a position of goodwill.

“Overall this looks good, but here are a few minor suggestions…”

That’s not a review. That’s a compliment with footnotes.

The AI accumulates context over a long conversation. It remembers that it wrote the code. It remembers the design decisions you discussed together. It has, for lack of a better word, a relationship with the codebase. And relationships create blind spots.

Verified Spec-Driven Development

VSDD comes from the open-source community — specifically from contributors dollspace-gay, gitcnd, and jtremback. It takes three methodologies that most teams treat as competing philosophies and stacks them as sequential gates in a single pipeline:

Spec-Driven Development (SDD): Define the contract before writing a line of implementation. Not just what the code does, but what must be provable about it.

Test-Driven Development (TDD): Write tests before code. Red, green, refactor. No code exists without a failing test that demanded it.

Verification-Driven Development (VDD): Subject all surviving code to adversarial refinement until a hyper-critical reviewer is forced to invent flaws because real ones no longer exist.

Three gates. Specs define what. Tests enforce how. Adversarial verification ensures nothing was missed.

Four Roles, One Pipeline

VSDD assigns distinct roles:

The Architect (Human Developer) — Strategic vision, domain expertise, and final authority. You sign off on specs, resolve disputes, and make judgment calls that AI can’t.

The Builder (Claude or similar) — Writes specs, generates tests, implements code, and refactors. Operates under strict TDD constraints. No “let me just write the whole thing and add tests after.”

The Tracker (Chainlink) — Hierarchical issue decomposition. Every spec, test, and implementation maps to a tracked unit of work. Nothing slips through the cracks.

The Adversary (Sarcasmotron) — A different AI model configured for zero tolerance. Reviews specs, tests, and implementation. Fresh context on every pass. No relationship drift. No accumulated goodwill.

The Adversary is the key innovation. Using a different model family (say, Gemini when Claude is your Builder) introduces genuine cognitive diversity. The Adversary doesn’t care about your feelings — it cares about your invariants.

The Six Phases

Phase 1: Spec Everything

Before any code, the Builder produces a behavioral specification and a verification architecture:

  • Behavioral contracts: preconditions, postconditions, invariants
  • Edge case catalog: null, empty, max size, negative, unicode, concurrent
  • A “purity boundary map” separating the deterministic core from the effectful shell (I/O, database, network)

That last point matters most. If your system has side effects woven through core logic, no amount of testing will make it formally verifiable. A function that reads from a database, calculates, and writes to a log in one block cannot be formally verified. But a function that takes data in and returns a result — that’s a function a model checker can reason about.

This boundary must be drawn at the spec level because it shapes everything downstream.

The Adversary reviews the spec before any tests are written. It looks for ambiguous language, missing edge cases, implicit assumptions, and contradictions.

Phase 2: Red, Green, Refactor

With an airtight spec, the Builder writes tests — only tests, no implementation yet. Every postcondition becomes an assertion. Every edge case becomes a test.

All tests must fail before implementation begins. If a test passes without implementation, something is wrong.

Then: pick the next failing test, write the minimum code to pass it, run the full suite, repeat. Classic TDD, enforced by AI.

Phase 3: The Gauntlet

The verified, test-passing codebase goes to the Adversary in a fresh context window.

The Adversary checks:

  • Does the implementation actually satisfy the spec, or did the tests encode a misunderstanding?
  • Are there tautological tests that would pass even if the implementation were subtly wrong?
  • Hidden coupling, race conditions, missing resource cleanup
  • Input validation gaps, injection vectors
  • Behavior that exists in code but isn’t covered by the spec

No “overall this looks good” preamble. Every piece of feedback is a concrete flaw with a specific location.

Phase 4: Feedback Loop

The Adversary’s critique feeds back through the entire pipeline:

  • Spec-level flaws → update spec, re-review
  • Test-level flaws → fix tests, verify they fail, fix implementation
  • New edge cases → add to spec, write failing tests, implement

This loop continues until convergence.

Phase 5: Formal Verification

Because the architecture was designed for verifiability from Phase 1:

  • Formal proof execution (Kani, Dafny, TLA+)
  • Structured fuzzing (AFL++, cargo-fuzz)
  • Mutation testing (mutmut, Stryker)
  • Purity boundary audit

Phase 6: Convergence

The system is done when four dimensions converge:

DimensionSignal
SpecAdversary’s critiques are nitpicks about wording, not missing behavior
TestsAdversary can’t identify a meaningful untested scenario
ImplementationAdversary is forced to invent problems that don’t exist
VerificationAll formal proofs pass, fuzzers find nothing

This is called “hallucination-based termination” — the adversary must hallucinate flaws because real ones no longer exist. The software is considered “Zero-Slop.”

Why This Matters for AI-Assisted Development

The key insight: AI politeness is a systematic risk. Using cooperative AI to review cooperative AI’s output is like asking your friend to grade your homework — they’ll find some mistakes, but they’ll miss the ones that require adversarial thinking.

VSDD uses AI against AI. Different model, fresh context, zero tolerance. That’s not just a code review — that’s a stress test.

And the human developer isn’t diminished by this process. You’re elevated. You’re the Architect — the strategic layer that approves specs, resolves disputes, and makes the judgment calls that no amount of AI can replace.

When to Use It (and When Not To)

VSDD is high-ceremony by design. Worth the overhead when:

  • Correctness is non-negotiable (financial systems, medical software, infrastructure)
  • The codebase will be maintained long-term
  • Security is a primary concern
  • Multiple AI models are available

For rapid prototyping or throwaway scripts, use the parts that make sense. Even adding just Phase 3 — an adversarial review pass with a different AI model — catches a surprising amount of hidden debt.

Getting Started

You don’t need to adopt all six phases tomorrow. Start with one:

Add an adversarial pass. After your next feature is “done,” open a fresh conversation with a different AI model. Give it zero-tolerance instructions. Paste in your spec, tests, and implementation. See what it finds.

Most developers who try this are uncomfortable with how much the adversary catches. That discomfort is the point. It means your current process has gaps, and now you know where they are.

Full Traceability

One of VSDD’s strongest properties: every artifact links back.

Ask “Why does this line of code exist?” and you can trace it back to a specific spec requirement. Ask “Why is this module a pure function?” and the answer traces to the Purity Boundary Map in Phase 1.

No line of code exists without a reason. No reason exists without evidence. That’s Zero-Slop.

References

  • Verified Spec-Driven Development (Original Gist) — dollspace-gay, gitcnd, jtremback
  • Kani — Rust Model Checker
  • Dafny — Verification-Aware Programming Language
  • TLA+ — Formal Specification Language
  • AFL++ — Fuzzing Framework
  • Stryker — Mutation Testing

item.title: /simplify and /batch: Two Claude Code Commands You're Probably Not Using
item.content:encoded:
/simplify and /batch: Two Claude Code Commands You’re Probably Not Using

You just finished a feature. Forty-seven files touched, tests pass, CI is green. You open a PR and your teammate leaves 23 comments — duplicated logic in three places, a helper function that already exists in utils, and an N+1 query hiding inside a loop.

Claude Code ships with two commands that prevent this. Most people don’t know they exist.

/simplify: Your Pre-PR Quality Gate

/simplify runs three specialized review agents in parallel on your recently modified code:

AgentWhat It Catches
Code ReuseDuplicated logic, redundant patterns, existing helpers you missed
Code QualityReadability issues, naming problems, structural concerns
EfficiencyPerformance bottlenecks, unnecessary computation, wasted work

After analysis, it aggregates findings and applies fixes automatically.

How to Use It

Run it after finishing a feature, after fixing a bug, before opening a PR. It catches structural issues that linters miss — linters check style, /simplify checks architecture.

What Happens Under the Hood

Three agents launch simultaneously. The Code Reuse agent scans for patterns that appear in multiple places and checks whether utility functions already handle that logic. The Code Quality agent reads your code the way a reviewer would — variable names, function decomposition, control flow clarity. The Efficiency agent profiles the logic for unnecessary allocations, redundant loops, and operations that could be batched.

They finish. Findings get merged. Fixes get applied. You review the diff.

That’s the whole workflow.

/batch: Parallel Codebase Migrations

Now for the bigger problem. You need to migrate 40 files from one API format to another. Or replace every use of lodash with native equivalents. Or add type annotations to all untyped function parameters.

File by file, this takes days. /batch does it in parallel.

Three Phases

Phase 1 — Research and Plan. /batch explores the affected files, decomposes the work into independent units (typically 5 to 30 tasks), and determines how to verify each one.

Phase 2 — Spawn Workers. One background agent per unit, each running in an isolated git worktree. Every agent implements its task, runs /simplify on its changes, executes tests, commits, and opens a pull request.

Phase 3 — Track Progress. A status table updates as workers complete, showing which PRs are ready for review.

Usage

Each worker operates independently. If one fails, the others keep going. Every PR is independently mergeable.

When /batch Works Well

  • Framework migrations
  • API contract updates across callers
  • Convention enforcement across a codebase
  • Dependency replacements
  • Test generation for untested modules

When It Doesn’t Fit

  • Tightly coupled changes that require coordination between units
  • Exploratory refactoring where you don’t yet know the target
  • Single-file modifications (just use /simplify)

Side by Side

Aspect/simplify/batch
ScaleRecently changed filesEntire codebase
TimingAfter implementationBefore implementation
Agent count3 parallel reviewers5-30 parallel implementers
OutputApplied fixes on current branchMultiple pull requests
Git requirementOptionalRequired (uses worktrees)

The Integration

Here’s the part that ties them together: every /batch worker automatically runs /simplify on its changes before committing. So each generated PR has already been through the three-agent review. Your human reviewers get cleaner PRs with fewer issues to flag.

Practical Workflows

Daily feature development:

Large-scale migration:

API standardization:

Getting Started

These commands are bundled with Claude Code. No installation, no configuration.

  1. Finish your next feature. Run /simplify. Look at the diff.
  2. Find a migration you’ve been putting off. Run /batch with a clear description. Approve the decomposition plan.
  3. Build the habit. /simplify before every PR. /batch for anything touching more than a handful of files.

The 23-comment PR is avoidable. These two commands make it avoidable by default.

Source: claudefa.st - /simplify and /batch Commands Guide

item.title: BMad v6.0.4 + GDS v0.1.10: Edge Case Hunter, Multi-Agent Testing, and Engine Knowledge
item.content:encoded:
Your Code Review Has a Blind Spot

Adversarial code review is good at catching design flaws, naming issues, and logic errors. But it has a gap. Branching paths with nested conditions - the ones where input values sit right at a boundary - tend to slip through. You know the kind: what happens when the array is empty? When the counter hits exactly zero? When the user passes null where a string was expected?

BMAD v6.0.4 and GDS v0.1.10 shipped together. Here’s what changed and why it matters.

Edge Case Hunter

The new Edge Case Hunter is a method-driven review tool. It traces every branching path in your code and checks boundary conditions systematically. It reports only unhandled gaps - no noise, no generic suggestions.

It works alongside adversarial review, not instead of it. Adversarial review catches structural and design problems. Edge Case Hunter catches execution path problems.

Here’s what happened during development: the first version forced the tool to always report findings. If it found nothing, it would halt and retry. The result? The AI fabricated issues. When you pressure an LLM to always produce output, it will produce output - even if there’s nothing to report.

The fix was to remove the zero-findings halt condition (#1797). Let the tool say “I traced all paths, nothing is missing.” That single change made the tool trustworthy. False positives destroy confidence faster than missed bugs.

Test Architect Enhancements

Test Architect got four upgrades:

Agent Teams - Coordinate multiple testing agents working in parallel. One agent handles unit tests, another runs integration tests, a third checks API contracts. They share context and avoid duplicating work.

Sub-agents - Complex test scenarios that would overwhelm a single agent can be decomposed. A parent agent defines the test plan, sub-agents execute individual test suites.

Configurable tool modes - Set the testing approach at input. Choose between strict TDD, exploratory testing, or regression-focused modes before the first test runs.

Pact.io contract testing - Service-to-service contracts built into the BMAD workflow. Define what your API promises, verify that consumers and providers agree, catch breaking changes before they reach production.

GDS v0.1.10: Engine Knowledge

Game Dev Studio now knows your engine. Knowledge bases were added for Unity, Unreal, Godot, and Phaser. Each one covers:

  • Performance optimization patterns specific to that engine
  • Coding conventions and best practices
  • Popular and useful third-party plugins
  • Licensing and cost details
  • Platform-specific caveats
  • When NOT to use this engine (honest guidance)

The game architecture workflow auto-loads the relevant knowledge fragment when you select your engine in Step 3. Steps 4 (Decisions), 6 (Structure), and 7 (Patterns) all use engine-specific context. No more generic “consider performance” advice - the system knows that Unity’s garbage collector has different constraints than Godot’s scene tree.

Credit to @sjennings for this contribution.

Brainstorming Fix

Previous behavior: start a new brainstorming session and your existing one gets overwritten. All that context, gone.

New behavior: the system detects an existing session and prompts you to continue it or start fresh. Small change, but anyone who lost brainstorming context will appreciate it.

Other Fixes

  • Installer templates (#1769): Legacy @ path prefixes replaced with explicit {project-root} syntax. More predictable, easier to debug.
  • Docs domain (#1777): Broken documentation domain references in README and issue templates are fixed.

The Numbers

5 commits, 3 PRs, 4 contributors: @lum (2 PRs), @alex, @chrischen, @BMad. Community-driven, solving problems people actually hit.

v6.0.3 Highlights (If You Missed It)

  • Root Cause Analysis skill (#1741)
  • Fixed nested directory installer (#1735)
  • OpenCode mode: all support (#1764)
  • CSV manifest quotes fixed (#1746)

Install

If you already have BMAD installed, the installer detects your setup and offers a quick update path.

Links

  • GitHub Releases
  • BMAD-METHOD Repository
  • BMadCode YouTube
  • Support the Project
  • GDS Module Repository

BMAD Builder is coming next.

item.title: BMAD v6.0.4: Two Minutes from Beta to Stable
item.content:encoded:
One Command, Two Minutes

You type bunx bmad-method install. The installer sees your beta 8 setup and offers a Quick Update. Two minutes later, you have a stable platform with 82 workflows, 27 agents, and configurations for 28 AI tools. Your existing settings are untouched.

That’s the whole upgrade story. But the details are worth knowing.

What Changed: Beta 8 to v6.0.4

BMAD v6.0.4 is the first official stable release. The “beta” label is gone.

The core and all five modules (bmm, bmb, cis, gds, tea) got updates. The TEA module is the big addition - it brings automated testing into the BMAD ecosystem. The tool count jumped from 18 to 28, covering nearly every AI coding assistant on the market.

Here’s what’s new:

WhatBefore (Beta 8)After (v6.0.4)
Release statusBetaStable
Modules56 (TEA added)
Workflows~7482
AI tool integrations1828
Contract testing-Pact.js + SmartBear MCP
Browser testing-Playwright CLI + MCP
Stack detectionManualAuto-detect
CI platform detectionManualAuto-detect

The Upgrade Process

If you already have BMAD installed, run:

The installer detects your existing installation:

Pick Quick Update. That’s the key decision.

The installer checks each module. In my case, all five existing modules were already structurally up to date - the update applied configuration refinements and the new TEA module setup.

TEA Module: Testing Built In

The TEA (Test Architect) module is the standout addition. During upgrade, it asks a few questions:

Pact.js contract testing - If you work with microservices, enable this. It integrates @seontechnologies/pactjs-utils for consumer-driven contract testing, with both local monorepo and remote broker workflows.

SmartBear MCP - Connects to PactFlow for broker queries. Requires PACT_BROKER_BASE_URL and PACT_BROKER_TOKEN as env vars.

Execution mode - TEA can orchestrate multi-step generation using agent-teams, subagents, or sequential fallback. The “Auto” option probes your runtime and picks the best mode.

Stack auto-detection - TEA reads your project files to determine if you’re running frontend, backend, or full-stack:

  • Frontend: package.json with React/Vue/Angular, playwright.config.*
  • Backend: pyproject.toml, pom.xml, go.mod, *.csproj
  • Full-stack: Both indicators present

CI platform auto-detection - Checks for .github/workflows/, .gitlab-ci.yml, Jenkinsfile, and other CI config files.

Playwright Integration

TEA includes Playwright setup for browser testing:

Two MCP servers can be configured:

  1. playwright - npx @playwright/mcp@latest
  2. playwright-test - npx playwright run-test-mcp-server

After the Upgrade

The final output tells the full story:

28 AI tools now have BMAD configurations. That includes the original 18 plus newer additions: Kilo, Kiro, OpenCode, QwenCoder, Rovo Dev, Trae, Antigravity, iFlow, and others.

The last line is the one that matters: preserved settings. Your name, language, output directory, module preferences - all carried over.

The Full Tool Support List

BMAD v6.0.4 generates configurations for:

  • Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, Windsurf (top tier)
  • Auggie, Cline, Crush, Gemini CLI
  • GitHub Copilot, Google Antigravity, iFlow
  • Kilo Code, Kiro, OpenCode, QwenCoder
  • Roo Cline, Rovo Dev, Trae

Each tool gets its own configuration directory with 27 agents, 74 workflows, and 7 tasks.

First Time? Start Here

If you’re installing BMAD fresh:

Follow the interactive prompts. Pick at least the BMM and BMB modules. Add CIS if you do creative work, GDS for game dev, TEA for testing.

After installation, run /bmad-help in your AI coding tool to see what’s available.

Resources

  • Docs: https://docs.bmad-method.org/
  • GitHub: https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD/
  • Changelog: https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md
  • YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/@BMadCode
  • Discord: https://discord.gg/gk8jAdXWmj

BMAD is 100% free. No paywalls, no gated Discord. If you find it useful, star the repo or subscribe to the YouTube channel.

item.title: Stop Arguing About Modeling: AMDD and the Art of Just Barely Good Enough
item.content:encoded:
Most agile teams I’ve seen fall into one of two traps. The first group spends three months drawing UML diagrams that nobody reads past page five. The second group interprets “working software over documentation” as “let’s just wing it” — then wonders why they’re rebuilding the authentication system for the fourth time.

Both groups are convinced the other group is the problem. They’re both wrong.

The False Choice

Software development has been stuck in this argument for decades. Model Driven Development (MDD) says: think everything through before you code. Comprehensive models, exhaustive specifications, detailed diagrams. Only then do you start writing code.

The agile counter-argument: that’s a fantasy. Requirements change. Markets shift. Customers don’t know what they want until they see it. Just build something and iterate.

Both contain truth. Both fail when taken to extremes.

MDD teams deliver detailed specs for systems nobody wants anymore. Agile teams without design discipline accumulate architectural debt until the system buckles. Both point at each other: “See? That’s what happens.”

What High-Performing Teams Actually Do

Watch a productive agile team — not the kind that just renamed their waterfall process. You’ll see something interesting. They don’t skip modeling. They don’t spend weeks on it, either.

A typical scene: two developers at a whiteboard. One draws a rough diagram of how a new service should interact with existing components. The other challenges an assumption. They debate for about ten minutes, erase half the diagram, agree on an approach, and go back to their desks to write code. They might do this three or four times during a sprint.

That’s Agile Model Driven Development. AMDD.

The Core Idea

AMDD’s central concept is captured in one phrase: just barely good enough.

Not “no models.” Not “perfect models.” Models that are sufficient to move forward and not one diagram more.

The lifecycle has four parts:

1. Envisioning

At the start of an initiative, spend a few days on high-level requirements and architecture. You’re answering two questions: “What are we building?” and “How might we build it?”

A whiteboard sketch of the architecture. User stories on index cards. A rough domain model. That’s enough to estimate costs and organize the team.

Don’t invest more than a couple of weeks here, regardless of project size. Going longer means you’re accumulating risk without the concrete feedback that real implementation provides.

2. Iteration Modeling

At the beginning of each sprint, model enough to estimate and plan. This is analysis and design for the sprint’s requirements, done during planning itself. Not a separate phase.

3. Model Storming

This is where most actual modeling happens. Two or three people, five to ten minutes, rarely more than thirty. Someone has a question they can’t resolve alone. They grab a colleague, sketch it out, resolve the question, and go back to coding.

Extreme Programming practitioners called these “stand-up design sessions.” The name doesn’t matter. The practice does: get stuck, grab someone, sketch, resolve, move on.

4. TDD as Specification

Test-driven development isn’t just about testing. Your tests are your detailed specification. Customer tests capture requirements. Developer tests capture design decisions. One source of truth that executes and validates itself. No separate specification document that goes stale the moment it’s printed.

Why Timing Beats Volume

AMDD works because of when you model, not how much.

On day one of a project, you know the least about the domain. Your questions are shallow. Stakeholders answer in abstractions because they haven’t seen anything concrete. Every assumption is unvalidated.

By iteration four, you’ve delivered working software three times. Stakeholders have used it. Your domain knowledge has depth. You ask better questions. Stakeholders give better answers because they’re reacting to real software, not theoretical specs.

JIT modeling focuses effort on what you’re actually building. Traditional upfront modeling often specifies features that never get used. That’s not planning. That’s waste.

The Uncomfortable Implication

AMDD changes who you need on your team.

If modeling sessions last ten minutes and coding lasts hours, a full-time modeler has nothing to do between sessions. The role doesn’t make sense.

What AMDD needs is the generalizing specialist — someone with deep expertise in one or two areas and broad competence across the lifecycle. They can model. They can code. They can test. They switch modes as the work demands.

This isn’t about being mediocre at everything. It’s about being excellent at your specialty while being competent enough to contribute wherever you’re needed.

AMDD vs. Other Approaches

vs. Traditional MDD: MDD creates extensive models before coding. AMDD creates minimal models throughout. MDD tends toward serial development; AMDD is iterative by nature.

vs. Feature Driven Development (FDD): FDD insists features are your primary requirement artifact. AMDD doesn’t specify which artifacts to use — it says use the right one for the situation.

vs. Use Case Driven Development: Same flexibility. AMDD is compatible with use cases, user stories, or any other requirements format. The message is the same: think before you code, but don’t overthink.

Getting Started

You don’t need a process overhaul to start doing AMDD:

  1. Before coding a feature, spend five minutes sketching the approach on paper or a whiteboard. Throw the sketch away when you’re done coding.
  2. When stuck on how two components should interact, grab a teammate and model storm it. Set a timer for fifteen minutes.
  3. Write tests first. Let them serve as your specification. Stop maintaining separate design documents that nobody updates.
  4. At the start of each project, spend a few days sketching the high-level architecture and key requirements. Enough to estimate. Not enough to be “complete.”

Most teams (70-80%) do this with whiteboards and sticky notes. That’s fine. The practice matters more than the tooling.

The Point

The debate between “plan everything” and “plan nothing” is a false choice. The answer: plan just enough, at the right moment, then get back to building.

Stop arguing about modeling. Start modeling at the right time.

References

  • Agile Model Driven Development (AMDD) — Scott Ambler’s original essay
  • Agile Modeling — The AM method home
  • Initial Requirements Modeling
  • Initial Architecture Modeling
  • Model Storming
  • Just Barely Good Enough Models
  • Generalizing Specialists

item.title: The Self-Driving Codebase: Background Agents and the Next Era of Software Delivery
item.content:encoded:
While most people slept, Cursor’s AI system generated roughly 8,000 commits. That was in a single eight-hour stretch. Over seven days, the system accumulated 10 million tool calls, running several hundred agents concurrently, with peak throughput of approximately 1,000 commits per hour. The humans in the room weren’t writing code. They were watching dashboards and calibrating direction.

That’s the self-driving codebase. It’s already here.

The Thing You’ve Been Calling a Bot Is Different Now

We’ve had automated code for years. Build servers. Linting pipelines. Dependency bots that open a PR when a library releases a new version. These are scripted systems. They execute predetermined steps.

Background agents are different in one meaningful way: they reason.

When a background agent gets triggered by a security disclosure, it doesn’t follow a flowchart. It reads the CVE, examines the affected code, generates a fix, runs tests against the fix, and creates a pull request explaining what it changed and why. The agent made decisions at each step. The humans review the output.

That gap — between executing a script and making decisions — is the entire story.

What Cursor Proved

Cursor published their self-driving codebase research in early 2026. The numbers are worth sitting with.

A multi-agent system ran for seven days without stopping. Peak throughput: roughly 1,000 commits per hour. Total tool calls: 10 million. The project was a web browser — not a toy demo. Hundreds of agents operated concurrently on real engineering work.

The architecture that made this work wasn’t obvious. Earlier attempts failed.

A single powerful model (Opus 4.5) lost track of what it was doing. It “frequently stopped to proclaim success despite being far from it.” Self-coordination with equal-role agents sharing state files reduced 20 effective agents down to 1-3 due to lock contention. Agents avoided complex tasks to minimize conflicts. Neither approach scaled.

What worked was a hierarchy:

  • A root planner that owned the entire scope but never wrote code
  • Recursive subplanners that subdivided work while maintaining ownership
  • Independent workers that operated on isolated repository copies and delivered handoffs
  • Information flowing upward without global synchronization

Individual failures didn’t cascade. The system was anti-fragile.

The system also accepted a small constant error rate. Fixing errors quickly across distributed agents proved more efficient than preventing all errors upfront. When Agent A broke the build, Agent B noticed and fixed it. Ultimate CI/CD — where the CI can fix the bugs it finds.

Three prompting insights that shaped the architecture:

  1. Constraints outperform instructions. “No TODOs, no partial implementations” worked better than prescriptive guidance.
  2. Avoid checkbox mentality. High-level intent with agent autonomy outperformed granular task lists.
  3. Quantify scope. “Generate 20-100 tasks” conveyed intent better than “generate many tasks.”

The researchers noted: “Agents have immense engineering skill but will follow instructions to the end, good or bad.”

The Infrastructure Story

Running a few agents on a laptop is not background agents. Enterprise-grade background agents need three things.

Isolated compute. Each agent gets its own sandboxed environment that spins up on demand and tears down when the task is done. No shared state that can corrupt between jobs. No resource contention between parallel workstreams.

Event routing. Something has to decide when to trigger agents. A new pull request. A security advisory. A failing test in CI. A scheduled dependency audit. The event routing layer watches for these signals and dispatches agents with appropriate context and scope.

Governance. Permissions and blast radius controls — what can an agent touch, what can it not touch. Audit trails for every action an agent takes. Human review gates via pull requests before anything merges. Failure isolation so one agent’s broken state doesn’t propagate to others.

The key safety insight: agents are as safe as their surrounding infrastructure. A well-governed agent with limited blast radius is safer than an engineer with unconstrained production access. The pull request remains the critical human review point. That doesn’t change.

The Role That’s Changing

The phrase from background-agents.com captures it well: developers move “on the loop” rather than “in the loop.”

In the traditional model, a developer is in the loop — writing every line, making every decision keystroke by keystroke. In the background agent model, developers are on the loop — they set direction, review outputs, calibrate agent behavior, and handle judgment calls that agents can’t resolve.

The skills that shift in importance:

Prompt engineering and constraint design. What constraints produce reliable agent output? “No partial implementations” is more effective than “finish the task fully.” Learning what to say — and what to leave unsaid — is a skill.

System architecture for agent work. How do you structure a codebase so agents can operate independently? Small, self-contained modules. Clear interface contracts. Minimal cross-cutting dependencies. These aren’t new principles. They become more important when agents are the primary workers.

Review at the right depth. An agent’s technically-correct solution can introduce the subtle wrong — an approach that compiles, passes tests, and still misses the intent. Developers who catch this are valuable. Developers who rubber-stamp agent output are not.

Trust calibration. Knowing when to let the agent run and when to intervene. Reading the signals that indicate an agent is off-track. Setting the right amount of autonomy for the task at hand.

The coding itself becomes one skill among many, not the defining skill.

What This Looks Like in Practice

Background agent use cases that are deployed today:

Dependency updates. An agent audits all dependencies on a schedule, generates PRs for safe minor updates, reads changelogs for major versions and flags breaking changes for human review. Not a bot opening issues — an agent that understands the upgrade.

CVE remediation. Triggered by a security advisory, the agent locates affected code, generates a fix, tests it, and creates a PR with an explanation of the vulnerability and the fix. Time from disclosure to reviewed patch PR: minutes, not sprint cycles.

CI/CD migration. A legacy build pipeline needs to move to a new platform. An agent handles the translation file by file, testing each step. The engineer reviews the plan and approves the output. No one has to manually rewrite 200 pipeline steps.

Test coverage expansion. An agent analyzes coverage gaps, writes tests for uncovered paths, runs them to verify correctness, and submits for review. Targeted coverage, not random test generation.

Linting and standards enforcement. New coding standards rolled out across multiple repositories. An agent applies them consistently, without the coordination cost of distributing the work across a team.

None of these require the agent to make product decisions. They’re all well-scoped, reviewable, and bounded. That’s the pattern: background agents handle the work that’s clear enough to specify and important enough to do consistently.

The Uncomfortable Implication

The agents will follow instructions to the end, good or bad.

Cursor documented this directly. Vague directives led agents to pursue obscure features. Explicit specifications improved outcomes. When a single executor received too many simultaneous objectives — plan, explore, research, spawn tasks, review code, merge outputs — it exhibited dysfunction: sleeping randomly, refusing to delegate, claiming premature completion.

This means the human’s job is to be a good architect of agent work. Structure problems so agents can solve them. Provide constraints that produce good outcomes. Set clear scope boundaries. Review with enough domain knowledge to catch the wrong that passes all tests.

The developer who does this well is more valuable than the developer who types faster.

Getting Started

You don’t need a full background agent platform to start developing these skills.

Use Claude Code, Cursor, or GitHub Copilot Agent mode to run longer autonomous tasks and practice reviewing the outputs. Notice when the agent produces technically correct but contextually wrong results — that’s the review skill you’re developing.

Structure your next project with cleaner module boundaries and clearer interfaces. Not because agents are reading your code yet, but because it’s practice for the architecture principles that make agent work reliable.

When you write prompts for AI coding tools, focus on constraints over instructions. “Never add new dependencies without explicit approval” is a constraint. “Think carefully before adding dependencies” is not.

Pay attention to the background agent infrastructure landscape. The three pillars — isolated compute, event routing, governance — are where the enterprise tooling is consolidating.

The Point

The self-driving codebase isn’t a future state. For specific workloads — dependency maintenance, security patches, standards enforcement, test coverage — it’s operational today.

The infrastructure question is: isolated compute, event routing, governance. Without all three, you have a powerful laptop assistant, not background agents.

The role question is: on the loop, not in the loop. Review, calibrate, direct. Architect agent work rather than execute it.

The positioning question is: which skills matter in this structure? Constraint design. System architecture. Deep review. Trust calibration.

The developer who learns to work this way has a larger effective output than the developer who optimizes for typing speed. The transition is already underway.

Reference Links

  • The Self-Driving Codebase — Background Agents and Enterprise Delivery
  • Towards Self-Driving Codebases — Cursor Research Blog
  • Self-Driving Codebases: Hacker News Discussion
